prompt: Play this short World 3 same-hand different-action chain.

steps.prompt: Step 1: Hero is on the button with QJo and the pot is unopened. Which compact preflop action fits best?
steps.feedback_correct_v1: Correct. In position with the pot unopened, this hand is good enough to open.
steps.feedback_incorrect_v1: Incorrect. With QJo on the button and nobody in yet, raising wins the initiative and uses late position, so passing up the open gives away the best value line.
steps.why_v1: The same hand can still be a clean open when the learner has late position and nobody entered the pot yet.

steps.prompt: Step 2: Keep the same QJo, but now the cutoff opened first and hero is on the button. Which compact action fits better?
steps.feedback_correct_v1: Correct. The same hand now continues more cautiously because the frame changed.
steps.feedback_incorrect_v1: Incorrect. Once the cutoff opens first, QJo is no longer starting the betting, so calling keeps position without inflating the pot into a marginal raise spot.
steps.why_v1: A stable framework changes the action when the context changes, not just when the cards change.

steps.prompt: Step 3: Keep the same QJo again, but now hero is in the cutoff and the pot is unopened. Which compact action fits best?
steps.feedback_correct_v1: Correct. Earlier position makes this same hand a fold instead of a comfortable open.
steps.feedback_incorrect_v1: Incorrect. The same QJo becomes a fold from the cutoff because earlier position leaves more players behind and removes the button advantage that made the unopened raise profitable.
steps.recap_v1: World 3 gets more useful when the learner can explain why the same hand sometimes opens, sometimes calls, and sometimes folds.
steps.why_v1: The point is not memorizing one answer for each hand. It is learning how position and the action frame reshape the first decision.
